revision.comment: Bot: mark 2 deleted QID(s) as DELETED_QID in ill templates [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24376438097|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:風流踊]]
[[File:Aronia Blossoms Screen 2.jpg|right|thumb|500px|''Hanami Blossoms Screen'' by {{ill|Kanō Naganobu|en|Kanō Naganobu|qid=Q11571493|lt=Kanō Naganobu|1=Kanō Naganobu}} (狩野長信), depicting Furyū Odori during the {{ill|Azuchi–Momoyama period|en|Azuchi–Momoyama period|qid=Q319531|lt=Azuchi–Momoyama period|1=Azuchi–Momoyama period}}.]]
'''Furyū Odori''' (風流踊) or simply '''Furyū''' (風流) is a type of [[Japanese traditional performing arts|medieval Japanese performing art]] featuring groups of performers dancing in elaborate costumes or disguises to the accompaniment of instruments like [[:en:Gongs|Gongs]] (鉦), {{ill|Drums|en|Drums|qid=Q3715553|lt=Drums|1=Drums}} (太鼓), and flutes, as well as singing or chanting.<ref name="nishi">Nishikado (2004)</ref> It was especially popular during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}. 

This art form embodied the aesthetics of elaboration and beauty, capturing the attention of audiences with its visual appeal.

== Overview ==

Furyū Odori involves performers dressing in colorful or {{ill|costume|en|costume|qid=Q9053464|lt=disguised|1=Costume}} attire, dancing to the rhythms of gongs, drums, and flutes.<ref name="nishi"/> Over time, the term ''furyū'' expanded to include not only the dances but also the processions of splendid [[Matsuri float|Floats]] (山車) and the dances performed around them. This performance art has roots in [[Yōgō ritual dances|rituals against plagues]], [[Nembutsu chanting|Buddhist chanting]], and {{ill|Dengaku|en|Dengaku|qid=Q3022455|lt=ritual agricultural performances|1=Dengaku}}.<ref name="nishi"/> 

Following the turbulent {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} (1467–1477), Furyū Odori gained momentum, spreading widely as a performance art centered around dancing. A historically significant example is the depiction of Furyū Odori in the ''[[Toyokuni Festival Screen]]'' during the {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|qid=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} era (1596–1615), commemorating the 7th anniversary of {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}}'s death at {{ill|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|en|Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)|qid=Q709822|lt=Toyokuni Shrine|1=Toyokuni Shrine (Kyoto)}}. 

During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} (1603–1868), the emphasis on ephemeral aesthetics diminished, and Furyū Odori transformed into fixed rural traditions.<ref>Sasaki (2003), pp. 238–251</ref> Many dances were incorporated into agricultural rituals like {{ill|Rainmaking|en|Rainmaking|qid=Q7284934|lt=Rainmaking|1=Rainmaking}} and became favored by the ruling {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|qid=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} class. Despite many Furyū Odori traditions disappearing, some, such as those in {{ill|Iga Province|en|Iga Province|qid=Q1047128|lt=Iga Province|1=Iga Province}} ({{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|qid=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}}), have been preserved.<ref>Sasaki (2003), pp. 238–251</ref>

Furyū Odori served as the precursor to numerous [[folk performing arts]] and [[:en:Festivals|Festivals]], including [[Nembutsu Odori]], [[:en:Bon Odori|Bon Odori]], [[:en:rain dances|rain dances]], and [[insect warding rituals]]. Other derivatives include [[Taiko drum dancing]], [[:en:kenbu|kenbu]] (sword dancing), and Buddhist-inspired [[Hotoke-no-mai]].<ref name="nishi"/><ref group="note">Eastern Japan's lion dances and [[deer dances]] (鹿踊) also trace their origins to Furyū Odori. Nishikado (2004)</ref>

In February 2020, the [[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] recommended Furyū Odori as a candidate for the {{ill|Intangible cultural heritage|en|Intangible cultural heritage|qid=Q84036549|lt=Intangible cultural heritage|1=Intangible cultural heritage}} list.<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.bunka.go.jp/koho_hodo_oshirase/hodohappyo/92050401.html|title=Proposal for UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Registration|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|date=2020-02-19|accessdate=2020-02-19}}</ref> By November 30, 2022, Furyū Odori was officially recognized as part of the list, encompassing 41 events across 24 prefectures.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://www.asahi.com/articles/ASQCZ457RQCYUCVL02Z.html|title=Furyū Odori Added to UNESCO List|newspaper=Asahi Shimbun Digital|date=2022-pp-30|accessdate=2022-pp-30}}</ref> Despite this recognition, Japan's total number of intangible cultural heritage registrations remained at 22, as this was a collective registration.<ref>{{Cite news|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/2022pp01/k00/00m/040/037000c|title=Furyū Odori Recognized by UNESCO|newspaper=Mainichi Shimbun|date=2022-pp-01|accessdate=2022-pp-30}}</ref>

== List of Registered Events ==

; {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}

{{Notelist}}
<references group="note"/>

=== Sources ===
{{Reflist}}

== Further Reading ==
* Nishikado, Masahiro. "Furyū" in ''Encyclopedia Nipponica.'' Shogakukan, 2004. ISBN 4099067459.
* Sasaki, Seika. ''The World of Oral Traditions.'' Tokyo: Miyai Shoten, 2003. ISBN 4838231229.
